data.title: U.S. Jobs Report Tops Expectations
data.description:
U.S. hiring beats forecasts in January

The January U.S. jobs report, published on Wednesday, surprised to the upside. Payrolls rose by 130,000, a clear step up from December's revised total of 48,000 and well above consensus expectations. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate fell to 4.3%, a historically low level and a sign the labor market remains resilient.

However, the composition of job growth tells a more nuanced story. Gains were concentrated in healthcare and social assistance, with the construction sector also contributing. Meanwhile, higher-paying sectors such as financial services and parts of the information sector saw modest job losses. Those areas are typically associated with stronger wage growth and white-collar demand.

From a U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) perspective, continued job growth and a stable unemployment rate support rates staying on hold for now. If employment is still expanding, it's harder to argue that policy is meaningfully restricting activity. Markets did react, though, with gold extending its recent pullback as the stronger data tempered expectations for near-term rate cuts.

In short, U.S. job growth last month was better than feared. Still, where those gains occurred leaves some uncertainty around the broader outlook.

Markets embrace Japan's growth push

In Japan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi and the ruling bloc secured a supermajority in the country's Feb. 8 elections. This gave Takeichi's party a mandate to pursue stimulus, tax relief for consumers, investment in strategic industries, and higher defense spending.

In the wake of the election, questions quickly turned to funding. Japan's public debt already exceeds 200% of GDP (gross domestic product), leading investors to wonder if additional spending could lead to more borrowing, higher bond yields and a weaker yen.

The market reaction, however, was more constructive than cautious, with Japanese equities surging to new records. Meanwhile, government bond yields edged slightly higher, while the yen firmed modestly. This combination suggests investors are embracing the potential growth impulse while monitoring fiscal dynamics.

Tech leadership faces closer scrutiny

After leading markets last year, technology shares have lagged as investors rotate away from the most expensive AI-related names. Concerns around valuations, spending plans, and growth assumptions have intensified, particularly following recent capital expenditure announcements.

The largest technology firms are on track to spend more than $650 billion this year, representing a 60% increase from 2025. That scale of investment has sharpened investor focus on returns and execution. Investors are increasingly asking who benefits and who faces disruption. Three key themes stand out:

  1. Large, established software platforms retain structural advantages. Many are deeply embedded within enterprise systems. AI may expand functionality and spending over time rather than replace these platforms outright.
  2. Selectivity is increasing. Investors are gravitating toward businesses that are harder to replicate, including infrastructure and mission-critical back-office tools.
  3. Risks are becoming clearer. If AI lowers barriers to entry or weakens pricing power, profit margins could face pressure. Companies that rely on seat-based pricing models may find it harder to sustain historical revenue growth.

For now, investors are watching practical signposts and asking questions like: Are companies deploying AI at scale? Is AI generating incremental spending? Can revenue grow without simply adding users?

The answers are still emerging. In the meantime, the market is acting like a judge at the Winter Olympics, where strong routines may draw attention, but medals are reserved for those who can actually execute.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer solicitation on or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Diversification and strategic asset allocation do not assure a profit or guarantee against loss in declining markets.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

data.title: Only 181k Total Jobs Gained For All Of 2025 After Massive Revisions Lower
data.description:
The analysis below covers the Employment picture released on the first Friday of every month. While most of the attention goes to the Headline Report, it can be helpful to look at the details, revisions, and other reports to get a better gauge of what is really going on.

Current Trends

The jobs report showed an increase of 130k jobs in January. This was a beat over expectations but would objectively be considered a weak report. More surprising is the Household Report, which showed a gain of 528k jobs. This is very strong, but it should be taken in context. Last January a similar event happened but to a greater extreme – the Household report showed 2.24M gained vs a 50k loss.

Figure 1: Primary Report vs Household Survey – Monthly

The Household Report has been underperforming the Headline Report for years. That changed in 2025. For the year, the Headline report showed a gain of 181k jobs. To repeat, that is total jobs gained for the year! The Household report showed 2.4M, but excluding the massive gains in January, the reports actually get quite close.

Figure 2: Primary Report vs Household Survey – Annual

The BLS publishes the data behind their Birth/Death assumptions (formation of new businesses). The data is through December of 2025 and December showed an assumed loss of 67k jobs.

Figure 3: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth-Death Assumptions – Monthly

For the year, the birth-death assumption is a positive 1.15M against negative actuals of -1.08M. This means all positive growth YTD is directly tied to the birth/death assumptions.

Figure 4: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth-Death Assumptions – Monthly

Digging Into the Headline Report

Unfortunately, despite being highly unreliable, the Headline report is the best data we have for the more recent periods. Furthermore, this is the data the Fed uses to shape its policy. The 130k jobs number was accompanied by a fall in the unemployment rate to 4.3%.

Figure 5: Change by sector

Jobs by Category

When looking at the last 12-month trend, only half of the categories were actually above trend. The big drivers of the gains came from Education and Health, which added 137k total jobs.

Figure 6: Current vs TTM

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the numbers.

Figure 7: Labor Market Detail

Revisions

This is the biggest story of the jobs report. After all the revisions, the job picture is significantly bleaker than the data originally showed. Not only was every month in 2025 revised down, but 3 of the months had negative growth. Can you imagine how the market would have responded in October to a net job loss of 140k jobs?

Figure 8: Revisions

Over the last twelve months, jobs have been revised down by about 105k per month and revised lower by 98.3k over the last three months! This means that over 1.2M jobs have vanished due to revisions.

Figure 9: Revisions

More Detail in the Household Survey

Another level of detail in the Household report shows full-time vs part-time job holders. The data in the Household report does show that full-time jobs are being added, which is good.

Figure 10: Full Time vs Part Time

Historical Perspective

The chart below shows data going back to 1955.

Figure 11: Historical Labor Market

The labor force participation rate is still well below the highs before the Global Financial Crisis. This month it increased slightly to 62.5%.

Figure 12: Labor Market Distribution

Conclusion

The US economy averaged job gains in 2025 of 15k a month. That is abysmal and pretty much tells you that the economy is in recession. Furthermore, remember the data point above that the gains have come entirely from the formation of new businesses. This means that even the very meager job gains in 2025 exist due to basic BLS assumptions of new jobs based on the formation of new businesses. The US economy is very weak, and the Fed is going to have to step in. This is why gold and silver continue to show strength despite the recent volatility.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UTF Vs. ASGI: Why A 6% Discount And Rate Cut Cycle Make UTF The Obvious Choice
data.description:
Investment thesis

In this article, I'm going to compare two popular CEF funds that invest in utilities. These are Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund Inc (UTF) and abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI). The relevance of the study is related to the fact that they have different investment strategies, though their investment practices are linked to roughly the same assets. Furthermore, based on my forecasts, I expect the upward trend in the technology market to continue in 2026, along with the AI supercycle. The utilities sector benefits from these developments, but also from the fact that the Federal Reserve is likely to continue its cycle of interest rate cuts. Because a fund such as UTF actively uses leverage (29.71%), its borrowing costs are directly affected by interest rates. Therefore, as interest rates fall, leverage becomes cheaper, leading to higher returns on the fund's portfolio. Meanwhile, the ASGI is a new type of CEF that focuses on unleveraged investments in modern infrastructure instead of using leverage.

Fundamental similarities between UTF and ASGI

The main thing UTF and ASGI have in common is their investment concept, with a focus on utilities. Lots of companies in this sector offer services that have inelastic demand. It is a definite advantage for funds investing in such assets, because cash flow generation is more stable (thanks to the more stable profits of utility companies involved in gas, electricity, water supply, and telecommunications). I believe that this investment concept of UTF and ASGI determines their focus on defensive assets, because utilities are the most stable sector of the US economy in the face of a potential crisis or recession. Indeed, during times of crisis, the consumption of utilities by Americans is always necessary to meet basic needs.

Also, the UTF and ASGI have a bunch of other features that make them similar for a lot of income-focused investors. Firstly, their main goal is to diversify globally by adding assets from other regions (not the US) to the portfolio. Secondly, both funds aim to generate consistently high returns, enabling them to pay dividends to shareholders every month, thereby making their shares popular with income-oriented investors. That said, this is where the fundamental similarities between UTF and ASGI end. While they are both CEFs with portfolios focused on utility assets, closer analysis of the funds reveals criteria that distinguish them.

Fundamental differences between UTF and ASGI

First and foremost, what sets these funds apart is their basic structure, and it's important to point out the difference in their portfolios. This is because UTF relies on a hybrid structure, with around 15-20% of the portfolio consisting of preferred shares and corporate bonds. The presence of these assets is important in terms of assessing the potential for generating high returns, enabling the fund to maintain dividends during periods of increased volatility in common shares. Most of the selected holdings are the largest companies in the industry. The UTF managers invest in leaders, dominated by electric power companies (about 30-35%). Important to note is that they represent the most defensive sector with regulated tariffs, whose value is growing due to increased demand for electricity.

ASGI has a completely different portfolio, because this fund strives to make its investments less concentrated, due to which the share of the top 10 assets is 26.7% (as opposed to 28.7% for UTF). The portfolio itself consists of a significantly smaller number of companies (with 55 holdings compared to UTF's 288 holdings). However, managers of the fund aim to have 100% of the portfolio consist of common shares, thus making it more sensitive to market volatility and changes in the capitalization of selected companies. As part of sectoral specialization, the majority of assets are related to transport infrastructure. The proportion of electric power companies is lower than in UTF. Another thing worth noting is that the ASGI portfolio includes assets related to data centers and cloud infrastructure, so it's more dependent on the ongoing AI supercycle. An additional risk factor for the ASGI portfolio is that the invested assets include less well-known companies with greater growth potential but also greater risks due to volatility.

In addition to portfolio composition, another key difference between the funds is their use of leverage. To be more precise, UTF uses leverage as a tool for its operations, while ASGI is a non-leveraged fund. Currently, leverage for UTF is 29.71%, while its cost is 4.83%. By using leverage, the growth phase of the market allows for higher returns. Considering that we are currently in a cycle of falling interest rates, levered costs will decrease, thereby automatically increasing returns. Thus, one could say that UTF has weathered a more difficult period of high interest rates, meaning that its investment appeal will only increase with each Fed rate cut.

The risks associated with changes in P/NAV are also worth discussing in more detail. Historically, the price of UTF has tended to trade around its fair value (NAV). The average P/NAV ratio over a 5-year period had a premium of 0.36%. If we take the last 5 years, UTF shares had a maximum discount of 0.91x and a maximum premium of 1.14x. UTF shares are currently trading at a significant discount, a definite plus for investors, since buying $1 of the fund's assets will cost 94 cents.

As a rule, the ASGI shares have been traded at a discount. Nevertheless, by 2026, the trend has shifted to a fund premium, as I believe, it is due to the significant growth in the price of the shares included in the ASGI portfolio. Over the last 5 years, this fund's biggest discount was 0.82x, with the premium hitting 1.08x. The fund's P/NAV ratio is now at an all-time high. This is a definite disadvantage for investors who are focused exclusively on generating passive income, since even now, it is not as profitable to pay $1.08 for assets worth $1 as it is with UTF. Furthermore, risk of a premium collapse remains, with the possibility of a return to NAV.

Also, the UTF and ASGI differ in terms of their dividend policies, since ASGI has the advantage of higher returns (9.97% vs. 7.00%). Meanwhile, their average annual dividend growth rate over five years is 41.53%. Literally just recently, on February 11, 2026, it was announced that ASGI would increase its payments from $0.21 to $0.22. Forward yield on such a payment is 10.94%.

Comparative table of UTF and ASGI

The table below provides a comparison of the main criteria that distinguish UTF and ASGI. Both qualitative and quantitative indicators are presented here.

Because the key difference between any CEFs is reflected in their total return figures, let's take a look at the difference between them. We'll do this by analyzing the last year, in which ASGI's total return was 39.53% higher. In doing so, these figures should be compared with the passive fund State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU), a benchmark for UTF and ASGI. For the past year, the UTF has outperformed the XLU slightly (by only 1.25%), but the ASGI, in contrast, is significantly ahead (by 39.78%). These are past data, however, and reflect this difference only in 2025, with historical data for 3 and 5 years not showing such an advantage for ASGI. It is also important to consider the difference in P/NAV ratios, with a 6% discount on UTF shares looking more attractive for a current purchase than a 7% premium on ASGI shares. Their combined P/NAV valuation difference is 13%.

Risks of the investment thesis

Given that I have chosen UTF for investment, it is important to analyze the risks associated with investing in this fund.

  • Firstly, I think it's important to consider the high leverage, the cost of which is directly dependent on interest rates. The Fed's decision to pause its rate-cutting cycle means that my expectations for lower leverage costs may not be met, preventing the fund from increasing its returns.
  • Secondly, as the UTF portfolio consists of traditional utility companies, these may be affected by the "green agenda" or frozen electricity tariffs. An important factor contributing to the positive outlook for the fund’s shares is the observed increase in demand for electricity, leading to higher tariffs. Nevertheless, to stabilize prices and alleviate discontent among the population and other businesses, regulatory authorities may suspend tariff increases by limiting prices, even if only for retail consumers, and this would negatively impact the margins of utility companies.
  • Thirdly, coming back to leverage, I should mention that the fund can invest in illiquid securities, which, generally speaking, are not subject to redemption and can make up a large share of investment portfolios. Therefore, at such high leverage, realising illiquid securities leads to financial difficulties that negatively affect the fund's solvency.

Conclusion

Therefore, results of the comparative analysis between UTF and ASGI reveal fundamental differences between them. In terms of cash flow stability, the UTF focuses on traditional utilities and hybrid returns generated, including through leverage. Its portfolio consists of heavier and more reliable companies, rendering it dependent on the trajectory of market capitalization changes in the utilities sector. Shares in ASGI allow investors to bet on more modern infrastructure, which includes AI digital infrastructure, power-saving technologies, and environmental infrastructure. This portfolio consists of smaller companies, the volatility of which is higher. As a result, given the accompanying conditions observed in 2025, its portfolio could grow rapidly. Currently, though, P/NAV shows UTF shares are more attractive, with a surprising 6% discount, whereas historically the fund traded close to par value. Conversely, the ASGI shares have moved from a historical discount to a record premium, rendering them overbought and not favorably priced for a current purchase. As such, the expectation of lower rates with cheaper leverage shapes my bet on UTF, leading me to recommend a "Buy" rating for its shares, with a "Hold" rating for ASGI.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Supreme Court Tariff Decision And Key Tests Ahead
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review – Volatile swings and deleveraging continue

Markets have been subject to particular waves of volatility in the past weeks, as years of upward trajectories and the recent acceleration are now questioning extreme valuations. The culprit? Artificial Intelligence.

What had been a boon for stocks is now coming back to bite the investor where it hurts, with investors realizing that AI won't just be an upside-only catalyst.

Productivity gains by AI are now turning into fears of destruction for many firms, industries, and their components – look at tech and software, straight-up bleeding since October.

High investments and capital expenditures that were seen as only positive are now being targeted by profit-takers and short-sellers, as investors question how long it will take to materialize into profits.

This comes amid times when OpenAI, the founder of ChatGPT, is under heavy scrutiny for its fast-paced cash burning, and it gets even more damaging when OpenAI is pretty much the firm that started this entire AI trend to begin with.

In other words, AI won't just allow markets to shoot higher eternally. Some firms and industries will face pain ahead as the global economy prepares for an existential restructuring.

If and when a large number of firms see their activities stolen by revolutionary technology, it could not only lead to massive layoffs and reduced consumption but also trigger cascades of entire industries unable to function correctly, resulting in lower profits and, in turn, an inability to repay their heavy debt levels.

This could quickly turn into a global liquidity and credit crisis – but for now, it's mostly fear: fear of creative destruction, which is for now still lifting global earnings to all-time highs.

To put things into perspective, the Dow Jones still reached a 50,000 all-time high just last week, and Nasdaq is still well below 10% of its record, so for now, it's only a turn in narrative – with stock indexes maintaining a broadly range-bound picture, it's essential to take a step back and not get too afraid too fast.

Markets are all about risk, so too little risk could prevent seizing good opportunities. And in any case, volatility is great, not for investors, but for traders who will enjoy more movement.

The narrative could also change quite suddenly, particularly after Friday's soft CPI print (2.4% vs. 2.5% expected), which could finally wake the soft-landing hypothesis, especially with non farm payrolls beating expectations on Wednesday.

A harsh reality is settling in, and traders will have to keep that in mind when trading: in the times to come, price action will dominate the narratives, so always look at the bigger picture (and check higher-timeframe charts) to stay ahead of what's to come.

Weekly Performance across Asset Classes

Most of the assets that got beaten down last Friday attempted a rebound in this week's action. The best examples of this are, of course, cryptocurrencies and metals, which suffered heavily last week and are up between 3% to 8% on the week.

What also really shifted is the sudden interest in US treasuries, which could launch a wave of repositioning. Bonds had widely been left out of the everything rally in the past three years, bullied by risk assets and elevated inflation, so keep a close eye to see if this changes.

Week Ahead: Global Macro is back

Asia Pacific Markets: RBNZ Rate Decision and Australian Employment

Traders are getting ready for quite a heavy week for APAC trading. Sunday will commence with the Japanese GDP for Q4 where traders will now spot whether the effect of Takaichi's appointment turned into a positive for the Land of the Rising Sun. Looking at how the JPY bounced last week, expect large reactions.

Monday will present the RBA Minutes, pretty important for FX traders, particularly considering that the Australian Central Bank is the only one back to hiking.

The Aussie will also see an important test on Wednesday with its employment figures, essential to track whether the last hike was just a one-off or the beginning of a new cycle.

Tuesday, however, brings the most important test for APAC traders, with the RBNZ rate decision. It will be the first press conference for Anna Breman, the new RBNZ Governor. She will also appear on Thursday, so make sure to listen to how she communicates for further clues.

A reminder that Antipodean currencies (AUD and NZD) have rallied quite severely since the beginning of 2026. They will both face a huge test next week.

Europe and UK Markets: Major data for Great Britain

Despite some key releases for the Eurozone and Germany (surveys and German CPI on Tuesday, key speeches and PPI on Friday), participants will keep focus on UK data. Similar to the US last week, they will face a confluence of high-tier releases.

Starting Tuesday, markets await their employment data, followed by UK inflation data (CPI, PPI and Retail Price Index) before concluding the week with retail sales on Friday.

The releases are all during the overnight session in North America (2:00 A.M. ET), so make sure to keep your risk in check. And of course euro traders will have to stay aware of Lagarde's speech on Sunday (early) morning.

North American Markets: US Core PCE, Supreme Court Decision on Tariffs and Canadian CPI

North American traders will also have some work to do next week. Not mentioning the few speeches (keep an eye on Bowman on Monday), the week really starts on Tuesday with Canadian CPI.

Swap traders are aware that any beat there could significantly add pricing for hikes in Canada for the year to come, particularly after this week's BoC Minutes, where it was noted that the current 2.25% rate is on the "stimulative side".

Turning back to the US, FOMC minutes will be presented on Wednesday, and while they are not expected to be a market mover, they could provide interesting views on inflation, particularly considering how this week's CPI release changed the narrative.

In any case, traders will have to wait for Friday before seeing the release of major data, with a streak of US releases. The weekly closing session will commence with Core PCE (where markets await to see if there is any real improvement in the Fed's favorite inflation tracker).

Promptly after, US services and manufacturing PMIs will be on deck. It has been announced just this Friday morning that the Supreme Court will give the decision regarding Trump's tariffs next Friday. This will be a major market mover.

Even after the latest ease in narrative, keep a close eye on geopolitical developments, particularly those involving the US-Iran talks, as they are likely to continue influencing commodity and broader markets.

Next Week's High Tier Economic Events

Friday 13 Market Wrap

Friday's session brought quite a shift in the market, with the inflation report allowing struggling stocks, cryptos, and metals to rebound. Stocks are however closing unchanged despite the very positive CPI report – this could be a rough sign for bulls.

The best outperformers there are, of course, Ethereum and Bitcoin, which are both coming from very far after last week's tumble. With FX remaining fairly muted, the US dollar will be key to watch next week as traders await a repricing of FOMC cuts in 2026.

If it doesn't reach new lows next week, this sends a sign of relative strength for the greenback, particularly with the upcoming decision on tariffs.

Safe trades, and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

data.title: Marvell Technology Q4 2026 Preview: Cooling Growth And Guidance Makes Sense
data.description:
Setting The Stage

Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) is standing at a pivotal position as it prepares to report its Q4 2026 and FY 2026 earnings on 5th March 2026. Being a provider of data infrastructure semiconductors, the company has been riding the AI wave through its custom silicon, electro-optics, and interconnect solutions. However, the last three quarters have shown signs of moderating growth momentum amid broader semiconductor cyclical dynamics.

The stock is trading at about $78.23, down from its 52-week high of $112.5 and losing close to 26% over the last year to underperform the S&P 500. I believe this performance stems from the company’s cooling growth momentum over the last couple of quarters, which I will delve into details shortly.

As we approach the Q4 earnings release, investors are likely to be weighing whether the upcoming report could reignite momentum or near term headwinds such as decelerating growth rates and softer guidance will keep pressure on the shares. With this background, I rate this stock a hold as we wait to see how the upcoming report turns out to be.

A Recap To Recent Performance: Solid But Slowing Momentum

Following what was a strong upward revenue growth momentum building from Q1 2025, where the company had a growth rate of -12.17%, the uptrend lasted until Q1 2026 with a YoY growth rate of 63.26%. The company turned its negative growth rate in Q1 and Q2 of 2025 to a solid double digit growth, which appears to have peaked in Q1 2026, a point at which it started trending downwards to the current 36.83% in Q3 2026.

In my view, this trend is more or less a relative deceleration from peak explosive growth rates rather than an outright stagnation. I tend to believe that explosive growth rates in the north of 50% are unlikely to be sustained in the long run. I think a growth rate in the region of 20%-35% is sustainable and within the mid 20% semiconductor industry long term projected growth rate. In my view, the current double digit revenue growth rate is attractive, and the market could be judging this stock unfairly.

Compared to its peers, I find MRVL to be doing better than them. For example, its trailing YoY revenue growth of 44.95% is better than QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM) at 10.25%, and whose quarterly revenue growth has been on a consistent downtrend over the last six quarters from 18.69% in Q4 2024 to 5% in Q1 2026. Compared to Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) with a trailing YoY revenue growth of 23.87%, it follows that MRVL's revenue growth is significantly above its peers and thus superior to theirs. I view the recent quarterly YoY growth as conformity to the normal growth curve, where explosive growth is unsustainable in the long run.

When I look at profitability, I see that MRVL, despite having the lowest operating margin of the three companies, has its margin on an uptrend compared to the peers whose margins seems to be relatively flat, or at the very best, improving at a lower rate than MRVL. In my view, this signals its improvement in operational discipline. As for the net income, its most recent net margin stands at about 91.7%, way above its peers and a significant jump from the region of 10%.

However, it should be noted that this was primarily due to a one-time gain from divestiture. This resulted in a $1.8 billion pre-tax gain from the sale of its automotive Ethernet business to Infineon Technologies, pushing its Q3 2026 net income to about $1.9 billion.

Generally, MRVL has the strongest revenue growth compared to its peers, and the current decline in revenue growth, in my view, is normal, as explosive growth isn’t sustainable. I believe its lower profit margin is due to its lower scale compared to its peers, but having it improve greatly signals it’s catching up. When I talk of scale, MRVL with a trailing total revenue of $7.79 billion, it lags its peers, whose trailing revenue averages more than $50 billion.

This implies that its peers enjoy better economies of scale and hence their better profit margins.

Q4 2026 Expectations & Focus Areas

Recall the Q4 2026 guidance where management guided revenue of about $2.2 billion, implying about 21% YoY growth. This marks a notable drop from the Q3 2026 growth of 36.83%, meaning the management expects its growth rates to keep normalizing, perhaps to the range I had provided earlier consistent with the long term industry growth rate. On the other hand, the consensus revenue estimate stands at $2.21 billion, marking a YoY growth of 21.52%. This is within range with the management’s guidance and in line with my expectation that growth will normalize to about 20%-35%, which is relatively sustainable. This can be further supported by the consensus estimates stretching all the way to 2028 being within my estimated range.

Historically, MRVL has missed its consensus estimates only once over the last 6 consecutive quarters, which sets a good precedent going to Q4. Assuming its last 4 quarterly beat average of 0.58%, I am estimating a revenue target of about $2.22 billion.

On the other hand, its consensus EPS estimate stands at $0.79, marking a YoY growth of 31.36%. This signals the company’s improving profitability. Just like the revenue, Marvell has beaten 6 of its last seven consensus quarterly estimates, making it highly likely to beat its current estimate. Assuming it lasts 4 quarterly average EPS surprises of 1.3%, my EPS projection for Q4 2026 is $0.8. In general, I expect the quarter to be marked by lower revenue growth in line with my growth expectation and industry trends, both revenue and EPS beats, and improved profitability.

As for the key focus areas, I urge investors to keep a close eye on data Centre & AI momentum. This remains to be the company’s growth engine. In Q3 2026, Data Center revenue grew by about 38% YoY to $1.52 billion. Any commentary or updates on accelerating AI CapEx from cloud giants provide a major growth catalyst. Another important area of focus is the 2025 FY guidance. In my view, any revenue guidance in the region of 20%-35% would be within my long term expected growth rate, and this would potentially mark the end of the stock’s volatility since growth could stabilize within that region. Lastly, let’s pay attention to margins. Any signs of further operating margins would support a bullish case, especially at a time when the top line growth is entering the sustainable zone.

Valuation

At about $78.23/share, Marvell is trading at a P/S of 8.76x, significantly lower than AVGO’s 25.16x and higher than QCOM’s 3.37x.

Of the three companies, only AVGO has a positive price return in the last one year of about 40%.

When I take this further and look at the revenue growth rates, MRVL’s trailing revenue growth of 44.95% tops, followed by AVGO at 23.87% and QCOM at 10.25%. Based on this metric, the conclusion is that MRVL is undervalued and warrants a P/S in the north of 25x since its growth is superior to that of AVGO. Let me look at it differently. Over the last three quarters, there is one thing that stands out. The outstanding feature is that, AVGO revenue growth has been on a consistent uptrend, from 20.16% in Q2 2025 to 28.18% in Q4 2025. This has been contrary to MRVL and QCOM, whose growth rates were on a downtrend. For example, MRVL growth has declined from 63.26% in Q1 2026 to 36.83% in Q3 2026, and QCOM’s from 10.35% in Q3 2025 to 5% in Q1 2026.

Given this background, it follows that the market sentiments for both MRVL and QCOM have been negative due to their weakening momentum in revenue growth, and this explains their negative price returns. While I believe having the lowest revenue growth, which is still trending downwards, justifies QCOM’s lowest P/S ratio, I hold a different view for MRVL. Unlike QCOM, Marvell's growth has been in unsustainable region, and I believe its current trajectory signals a stabilizing growth. By it being above AVGO's, I believe that a P/S in the region of 15x-20x is reasonable if the market attaches a premium in an upward revenue growth trajectory.

Risks

Marvell's main risk is customer concentration, with the company deriving a substantial portion of its revenue from a limited number of customers. It is estimated that the top 10 customers account for about 81% of its total sales. In case of slowdowns, delays or reduction in spending by any of these customers, the company’s financials could be impacted materially.

Conclusion

As we approach Q4 2026, this quarterly report and the 2027 guidance will test whether the stock’s recent pullback represents a buying opportunity. From where I sit, the company’s cooling growth rates and management guidance are a reflection of a maturing business whose growth rates must normalize. As such, should the Q4 revenue growth rate and management guidance fall within the 20%-35% with a positive market outlook, I believe that would present a buying opportunity.

data.title: NFIB Small Business Survey: Optimism Remains Above Historical Average
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index inched down 0.2 points to 99.3, remaining above the index's historical average for a ninth straight month. This was below the forecast of 99.8 and puts the index at the 50th percentile of the series.

"While GDP is rising, small businesses are still waiting for noticeable economic growth. Despite this, more owners are reporting better business health and anticipating higher sales." - NFIB Chief Economist Bill Dunkelberg

Key Takeaways from January's report:

  • The cost or availability of insurance as the top problem for small business owners was at its highest level since December 2018.
  • The percent of small business owners who reported capital outlays in the last six months was at its highest level since November 2023.
  • The percent of owners who reported paying a higher interest rate on their most recent loan was down from December, suggesting credit markets are turning more favorable for small borrowers.
  • The percent of small business owners reporting labor quality as their top problem fell for a third consecutive month.
  • Actual price increases fell but remain well above the historical average, suggesting continued inflation pressure, while planned prices increased
  • The net percent of owners expecting higher real sales volumes over the next quarter increased.
  • The net percent of owners reporting inventory gains reached its highest level since January 2023.
  • Overall health of business evaluations: Excellent 14%, Good 54%, Fair 27%, Poor 4%

The first chart below tracks the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index since 1986, with a baseline level of 100. Notice the sharp declines in sentiment during the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic. In contrast, the index showed relative resilience during the 2000-2003 Tech Bubble collapse. Following the Great Recession, small-business sentiment remained weak for an extended period, a pattern that closely resembled the past two years of pandemic-related disruptions and high inflation. Another interesting pattern to the index was the nearly identical jumps following the 2016 and 2024 elections.

The average monthly change in the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is 1.4 points. To filter out short-term fluctuations, the chart below presents a 3-month moving average alongside the individual monthly values, represented by dots.

NFIB Small Business Survey Components

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is composed of 10 components. In January, three components increased and seven declined.

Business Optimism and Consumer Attitudes

The next few charts are overlays of the Business Optimism Index with two of the main measures of consumer attitudes: Conference Board Consumer Confidence and University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. The Consumer Confidence Index is influenced by employment and labor market conditions from the worker's perspective, whereas the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on employment conditions from the business perspective. (For more information on how these indexes measure up against each other, check out our monthly update, Two Measures of Consumer Attitudes: MCSI vs. CCI).

In our first chart comparing the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index, we can see that the consumer measure is the more volatile of the two. Therefore, it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

Next, we compare the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Again, we've plotted each index on a separate axis; however, in this chart, we can see that the business measure is more volatile of the two.

Despite the volatility, though, we can see that these two measures of mood (business and consumer) have been highly correlated, falling and rising together for the most part. A decline in Small Business Sentiment was a long leading indicator for the first two recessions of the century, but clearly not for the COVID-19 recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Copper And The Materials Behind Global Electrification
data.description:
Electrification, AI and clean energy are reshaping global infrastructure. Copper and critical metals are foundational inputs driving long-term demand across grids, data centers and EVs.

Metals Powering Electrification: Copper and the Critical Materials Behind a More Electric World

The global economy is becoming more electrified, more automated and more data-driven. While these shifts are often discussed in digital terms, i.e., artificial intelligence (AI), cloud computing and clean technologies, their foundation is physical. Electrification, AI and data centers all depend on metals that enable power transmission, connectivity and reliability at scale.

As a result, the future of infrastructure is increasingly a materials story. At the center of that story is copper, supported by a broader group of critical metals essential to electrification.

Electrification Is Driving Structural Metals Demand

Electrification extends far beyond electric vehicles (EVs). It includes power generation, grid modernization, energy storage, industrial automation and charging infrastructure. Compared with fossil-fuel-based systems, electrified technologies tend to be more materials-intensive, particularly when it comes to conductive and energy-related metals.

Copper plays a foundational role due to its electrical conductivity, durability and efficiency. It is widely used across electrified systems, including:

  • Power transmission and distribution networks
  • Electric motors, transformers and wiring
  • Renewable energy installations
  • Electric vehicle charging infrastructure

As electrification expands, copper demand grows not only because more infrastructure is being built but because electrified systems require significantly more copper per unit of energy delivered than conventional alternatives.

Grid Expansion and the Rising Role of Copper

Modern power grids are becoming larger, more interconnected and more complex. Integrating renewable energy, supporting distributed power sources and meeting rising electricity demand all require extensive upgrades to transmission and distribution networks.

Each of these upgrades increases copper consumption through new transmission lines, substations, transformers and grid-scale energy storage. As global electrification accelerates, grid expansion is expected to remain one of the most significant long-term drivers of copper demand.

AI, Data Centers and Physical Infrastructure

The rapid adoption of AI has accelerated global investment in data centers, facilities that require reliable power delivery, advanced cooling systems and dense electrical interconnections, directly increasing metals demand.

Copper remains essential for power distribution, cabling and cooling systems within data centers. Aluminum is widely used for structural components and heat management due to its lightweight properties. Meanwhile, specialty and minor metals, such as gallium and germanium, play critical roles in semiconductors, optical components and advanced chips that support AI workloads.

As data centers scale in size and computing intensity, metal usage per facility has increased, reinforcing the link between digital growth and physical infrastructure.

Electric Vehicles, Clean Energy and Critical Metals

Electric vehicles are materially different from internal combustion engine vehicles. EVs typically require several times more copper due to electric motors, inverters, wiring harnesses and charging systems. Clean energy sources like wind and solar also require a range of critical metals.

  • Lithium, nickel and cobalt, which influence battery energy density and longevity
  • Manganese, used in certain battery chemistries and wind turbines
  • Rare earth elements, essential for high-performance permanent magnets

Together, these materials form the core inputs for electrified transportation and clean energy systems.

What Drives Copper Prices

Copper prices are influenced by a combination of structural and cyclical factors. Long-term demand growth tied to electrification, renewable energy and digital infrastructure is a key structural driver. On the supply side, mine production, declining ore grades and development timelines play an important role.

Macroeconomic factors, including global growth trends and inflation, can also influence copper prices. Because copper is embedded across construction, manufacturing, energy and technology, it is often viewed as both an indicator of economic activity and a beneficiary of long-term electrification trends.

Studies have shown that copper demand is expected to grow from 28 million metric tons per year in 2025 to 42 million metric tons by 2040, an increase of 50% above current levels.

Chart 1: Global copper demand by sector (2025 – 2040)

Million metric tons copper (MMt Cu)

1Includes copper demand from construction, cooling, appliances, fossil power generation, machinery and internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles. 2Includes copper demand from clean energy technologies, transmission and distribution (T&D) and EVs.

Supply Constraints and the Importance of New Investment

Copper is not scarce in absolute terms, but expanding supply has become increasingly challenging. New mine development is capital-intensive and can take many years from discovery to production. At the same time, declining ore grades mean more material must be processed to produce the same amount of copper.

Similar supply challenges exist across other electrification metals, many of which have geographically concentrated supply chains. These dynamics can limit how quickly supply responds to rising demand, increasing the importance of sustained investment and innovation across the mining and processing ecosystem.

Chart 2: Copper’s Looming Supply Gap

Copper exposure is significantly important to the VanEck Copper and Green Metals ETF (EMET).

Chart 3: Highlights the weights of the metal exposures based on revenue generation in the fund

Positioning for a More Electrified Economy

Electrification, AI and data center expansion are often viewed as separate themes, yet they share a common reliance on metals that enable power, connectivity and performance. Copper plays an important role in this transformation, supported by battery metals, rare earth elements and specialty materials.

The VanEck Copper and Green Metals ETF highlights copper and critical electrification metals as essential inputs for modern infrastructure and long-term structural growth.

Important Disclosure

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

An investment in the Fund may be subject to risks which include, but are not limited to, risks related to investments in green metals, clean energy companies, regulatory action and changes in governments, rare earth and strategic metals companies, special risk considerations of investing in Asian, Australian, Chinese, African, South African, Canadian and Latin American issuers, Stock Connect, foreign securities, emerging market issuers, foreign currency, basic materials sector, mining industry, small- and medium-capitalization companies, cash transactions, equity securities, market, operational, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount, liquidity of fund shares, non-diversified and index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks. Small- and medium-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks. Investments in Chinese issuers may entail additional risks that include, among others, lack of liquidity and price volatility, currency devaluations and exchange rate fluctuations, intervention by the Chinese government, nationalization or expropriation, limitations on the use of brokers, and trade limitations.

Investments in companies involved in the production, refining, processing and recycling of green metals used to facilitate the energy transition from fossil fuels to cleaner energy sources and technologies are subject to a variety of risks. Under certain market conditions, the Fund may underperform as compared to funds that invest in a broader range of investments. There may be significant differences in interpretations of what is considered a "green" metal and the definition used by the Index Provider may differ with those used by other investors, investment advisers or index providers. Additionally, there may also be a limited supply of companies involved in green metals, which may adversely affect the Fund.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

data.title: TotalEnergies: LNG Exposure And AI Power Demand Offer Structural Growth
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered TotalEnergies (TTE), I reiterated their Buy rating, as they offer strong exposure to European oil and especially natural gas/LNG while advancing their cost savings program and adapting to the environment.

With a solid Q4 report and good performance throughout the year despite commodity weakness, TotalEnergies remains a Buy in my opinion, backed by strong financials and even a ramped-up cost savings plan, while they continue to be in a great position to benefit from future trends and their massive energy needs.

Internal Developments

TTE reported an overall solid Q4 and FY25, despite seeing their adjusted net income drop by ~13% in Q4, in line with the market’s expectations.

However, what’s even better is that TTE posted a very solid free cash flow of $10.39 billion in 2025, even though it dropped significantly from the $15.95 billion seen in 2024, with a lot of it being due to the ~$2.05 billion increase in CAPEX.

Unlike BP (BP), which fell when announcing the halt of their buyback program recently to focus on their balance sheet, TTE stock increased by ~4% on the day of their Q4 announcement, despite cutting their Q1 buybacks in half to $750 million for 2026, expecting between $3 billion and $6 billion for the full year while oil stands at $60 to $70, with the Q1 decision being based on oil prices around $60/bbl.

This way, similar to BP, they can focus on their balance sheet while also mentioning the possibility of stopping these buybacks entirely in case oil drops to $50.

They also maintained their dividend so far to the equivalent of a ~5.15% yield, but also mentioned the expectations of an increase by the end of April. That’s about $8.4 billion for the dividend (without accounting for an increase) plus at least $3 billion in dividends if oil prices remain around $60, bringing their total yield to roughly 7% in 2026 based on the current level, with the possibility for more depending on their dividend increase and better buybacks.

Meanwhile, they are planning on reducing their CAPEX to ~$15 billion (net) in 2026, with about 40% of that going towards new oil and gas projects and ~$4 billion going into integrated power, while also mentioning the flexibility to cut this further to $14 billion if oil prices drop below $50.

TTE increased their target of $12.5 billion in savings during the 2026 to 2030 period, which is a significant improvement from their previous $7.5 billion target, including $2.5 billion this year.

The company also estimates that with Brent at $60, $10/MBtu TTF and $5/b ERM, they could generate over $26 billion in cash flow, backed by accretive production growth and improved performance combined with their cost savings, meaning we could continue to see over $10 billion in free cash flow in 2026 as well despite the weakness in prices.

Financially, based on TTE’s latest report, we can see an overall solid position, with the current assets almost covering their current liabilities and a very manageable amount of debt, while also holding $26.2 billion worth of cash and cash equivalents, which can allow them to expand significantly even during a weak pricing environment.

The company also announced several projects and deals, including announcing the formal restart of their $20 billion LNG project in Mozambique (expecting a massive ramp-up in activity in the coming months), acquiring a 40% stake in Galp Energia’s (GLPEF) Namibia oil discovery, signing power contracts with Airbus (EADSF), and even a 15-year contract with Google (GOOG) to provide solar power for their data centers in Texas, which further shows this company’s long-term potential, among others.

Macro Developments

To briefly mention the situation in Venezuela and its impact - which I discussed in more detail in this Shell (SHEL) analysis published recently - I don’t see it as such a big issue for TTE.

Right now, I believe Exxon (XOM) described Venezuela very well as currently being “uninvestable,” as any operations there still require massive investments due to the ancient infrastructure, as well as a lot of refining since we’re talking about one of the heaviest oils on the planet, on top of the weak environment that pushed these companies into a CAPEX-cutting territory, as we saw with TTE as well.

Even with the country working on an overhaul aimed at easing state control of its oil industry, there’s nothing that will guarantee that these companies won’t lose their investments a few years later when Trump’s term is over - which wouldn’t be the first time - so these companies will very likely approach this very carefully, while ConocoPhillips (COP) recently said that their priority there is getting the money they are owed rather than spending more.

To add to this, we saw J.P. Morgan’s warning that oil could fall into the $30s by late 2027 if we see a massive oversupply - which these companies are very well aware of - while more stable scenarios still estimate WTI prices around $53/bbl to $54/bbl in 2026 and 2027, which is far from the best.

This can threaten TTE’s buybacks (note that they say $60 for Brent, but if WTI falls there, Brent would likely be below $60 too), further forcing these companies to reduce their CAPEX and growth ambitions while focusing on preserving their current operations.

Still, the fear and uncertainty surrounding the US due to all the recent geopolitical pressure, Japan’s economic plans potentially hurting its treasuries and further affecting the USD, among others, combined with renewed fear around Iran following Trump’s recent statement could actually turn out to be a positive for TTE, which can stand to benefit from a potential closing of the “European Discount” gap, as I mentioned for a couple of peers before.

Recently, TTE took full ownership of their Zeeland refinery in the Netherlands from Lukoil, as the latter began selling/losing international assets almost immediately after being hit by US sanctions. This is also an important factor to remember, as a potential resolution of the war in Ukraine could add more supply to the market as sanctions would gradually be lifted, potentially pressuring oil and gas prices.

As they show and I mentioned above, TTE can stand to benefit significantly from the rise in energy demand coming from data centers and AI, with >$250 million of EBITDA per year already secured and contracts with some of the world’s largest players, including Google, Microsoft (MSFT), Ascenty (the largest data center company in LATAM), ByteDance, Amazon (AMZN), and many others across the globe.

Still, Kepler recently downgraded TTE to Reduce from Hold back in late January (while upgrading BP), citing TTE's “structurally long LNG portfolio” as a weakness in an oversupplied LNG market, which is a valid concern.

However, this industry remains a solid long-term asset in my opinion, as it should still stand to benefit from Asian demand absorption and coal-to-gas switching, while the oversupply narrative ultimately relies on the assumption that many of these projects come online without delays, despite the current weakness in the environment (unrelated, but TTE's Mozambique LNG project has already faced multi-year delays), while TTE's assets also come with strategic importance.

Also, this call seems to be quite an outlier, as a wide range of analysts, including from UBS, Jefferies, RBC Capital, Barclays, and others have reiterated their Buy equivalent rating a few days ago, but only time will tell, of course, as we’re still talking about a company playing in a very volatile commodity market.

Risks Reiterated

As a commodity company, TotalEnergies will always depend on a very volatile environment, and there is certainly real risk that all of their commodities go through weaker environments before seeing anything better, which can lead to lower shareholder returns or even zero buybacks, which we’ve seen is not taken lightly by the market (see BP).

Meanwhile, even though a potential capital migration could benefit them, we have to keep in mind that if this comes as a result of weakness in the US, it can rapidly spread into a global problem, as the country remains key for the oil and gas markets from every point of view.

Valuation

For TTE’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll begin with the $10.39 billion of free cash flow reported in 2025 as the base, which seems like a fair assumption based on their expected Brent at $60, $10/MBtu TTF and $5/b ERM. Note that I’m not adding the positive effect of $3.83 billion in net borrowings - which would’ve made it the FCFE - nor do I adjust it for after-tax interest expenses, which would’ve made it the FCFF - as I want to capture their “true” average cash flow production potential.

This would then grow to $16.5 billion by 2030 (assuming the same commodity prices), which is a very conservative assumption despite their growth projects, Integrated Power expectations, massive cost saving program and also buybacks improving the per share results, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate given the solid potential coming from natural gas and energy generation going forward, with a discount rate of 11%, to take into account the volatility this industry comes with, as well as 2.18 billion shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $173.25 billion, for an intrinsic value of $79.47, which is still above the current $76.32 despite using a high discount rate that could easily be reduced if the market’s optimism, outlook or demand for a European major changes.

Conclusions

I reiterate TTE’s Buy rating, as the company remains in a strong financial position and is able to sustain a solid dividend yield, while standing to benefit significantly from long-term energy demand trends.

There are clearly risks to keep in mind, as we’re talking about a commodity company that’s exposed to some of the most volatile and politically-exposed commodities, but the overall risk-reward still seems to be in TTE’s favor.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Index Outlook: Some CPI Morning Bullishness
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The price action at the market open wasn't the prettiest one, but stock bulls haven't said their last words. After correcting by an average of -0.50%, equities are rebounding amid widespread bullish contagion, supported by the pricing of a third Fed cut in 2026.

It was surprising to see such a pessimistic reaction after this morning's print, given that inflation is the only data warranting higher rates in the US, which are currently still in the 3.50% to 3.75% range. Indeed, it is the softest inflation print in almost five years.

Still, the price action isn't so straightforward, with participants rattled by the brutal corrections, deleveraging and repricings from early February. What could rattle investors is that the cooling is not widespread, with a few factors still rising from the impact of tariffs – transportation services, which affect the entire pricing (with deliveries of goods), rose to their highest since Summer 2023.

In that regard, the US Supreme Court announced that it will issue its final decision on the legal status of the Trump tariffs on February 20, so keep that date on your agenda.

Stocks hadn't reacted to yesterday's announcement from Israel's PM Netanyahu that the US would really prefer a deal with Iran, which brought with it a wave of premium-reducing in commodities, with metals and energy commodities retracing.

Even cryptos are rebounding in this morning's action; hence, the widespread positive sentiment acted as a boon for equities.

Magnificent 7s and credit services (plagued by rising delinquencies) are the only laggards of this morning's action, while the rest are sighing in relief at the soft print.

Dive into today’s session intraday charts and key trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Dow Jones 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The DJIA tested its pivotal 49,000 support in this morning's action, with bulls leaning heavily on the psychological area for the ongoing rebound. Now testing its intraday pivot region, any push above this shouldn't see much resistance to regain 50,000 – watch the 200-hour MA (49,730).

Closing back above 50,000 this week should help sentiment throughout the rest of the month, so keep the level in check.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • January ATH key pivot and 200-hour MA 49,500 to 49,730 (testing)
  • 49,900 to 50,000 resistance
  • Intraday resistance: 50,250
  • ATH resistance 50,400 to 50,500
  • Index all-time highs: 50,512

Support Levels

  • CPI session lows: 49,073
  • Major support – 49,000
  • Past week support 48,600 to 48,700
  • Key support around 47,500
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 1H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is showing quite a strong rebound in this morning's action, pointing to a break-retest formation – a bullish signal. Nevertheless, bulls will have to push towards the 25,000 pivot to confirm the bounce, with the action stalling a bit towards the mid-session.

A close above the psychological level should help the tech sector for the weeks to come.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs 24,857 – bulls need to break for a higher push
  • Key pivot 25,000 to 25,250
  • 25,400 to 25,500 key intraday resistance (2H 200 MA)
  • Pivotal resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300

Support Levels

  • 24,500 to 25,600 key support (morning rebound)
  • February 5 lows: 24,165
  • October-November support 23,800 to 24,000
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is attempting a push above its pivot zone. Any break past 6,900 should easily lead towards some new all-time highs. Watch sentiment towards the close and Monday's open!

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Pivotal support zone 6,880 to 6,900 (watch the 50H MA at 6,900!)
  • Previous ATH resistance 6,945 to 6,975 (rejecting)
  • Session top: 6,996
  • Current ATH: 7,020
  • All-time high resistance: 7,000 to 7,020 (range highs)

Support Levels

  • Session bounce: 6,789
  • Mini support 6,830 to 6,850
  • 6,800 psychological support
  • Overnight lows 6,735 (range lows)
  • 6,400 Major psychological support

Safe trades, and keep a close eye on the US-Iran developments!

Original Post
